claim_text: ESIM couples the event simulator to the rendering engine and adaptively chooses the next render time so that the maximum expected brightness change or pixel displacement between samples is bounded.
quote:
It tightly couples the rendering engine and the
event simulator, which allows the event simulator to adaptively query visual samples (i.e. frames)
based on the dynamics of the visual signal (Section 3.3).

claim_text: Renderer-native event simulation computes events from the linear HDR color buffer without gamma correction, which is linear in light intensity and matches the response of real event-camera hardware.
quote:
Event Model

By default, events are computed from the renderer’s HDR
color buffer without gamma correction, which is linear in
light intensity and therefore matches the response of real
event-camera hardware.

claim_text: A physics-grounded event-camera plugin exists for NVIDIA Isaac Sim that generates events inside the simulator in real time using motion-vector-based frame interpolation between sparse rendered keyframes.

side a physics simulator.

The plugin implements a faith-
ful log-intensity contrast event model with per-pixel asyn-
chronous reference updates; it migrates from a normal RGB

camera with few changes and integrates into any Isaac
Sim / Isaac Lab scene, inheriting the simulator’s physics
It is fully configurable,
and frame-perfect ground truth.
and offers an interpolation option that renders only sparse
keyframes and synthesizes the in-between frames through
bidirectional motion-vector warping, making real-time gen-
eration on a single GPU possible.

claim_text: Monte Carlo path-tracing noise causes spurious events; statistical tests over log-luminance samples can distinguish genuine brightness changes from rendering sampling noise.
quote:
In ordinary Monte Carlo path tracing, the luminance
in units of W/(sr m2) is carried by a path sample, and the
luminance samples are integrated over the hemispherical
region of the camera sensor.

claim_text: Networks trained only on simulator-generated DVS data transfer poorly to real event cameras, attributed to event sparsity, missing noise realism, and temporal sampling uniformity of the synthetic streams.
quote:
Future work
should explore advanced domain adaptation methods, diverse architectures, and
differences in data characteristics, such as event sparsity, absence of realistic
noise, and temporal sampling uniformity in synthetic streams, which diverge
from the complex and asynchronous nature of real event cameras to further
bridge the sim-to-real gap and improve the practicality of synthetic data in real-
world neuromorphic vision systems.

claim_text: Sensor-realistic event simulators model stochastic voltage dynamics and circuit-level noise of the DVS pixel, and networks trained on such data generalize favorably on real events.
quote:
The evaluation on two tasks, i.e., semantic segmentation and
intensity-image reconstruction, indicates that neural networks trained
with DVS-Voltmeter generalize favorably on real events against state-of-
the-art simulators.

claim_text: Our snow simulation renders at 1250 fps and converts frames to events with v2e; per-frame particle evaluation makes rendering CPU-bound, and the AI denoiser must be disabled for the event pass because it fabricates brightness changes that become spurious events.
quote:
Event generation and merging

3.6 Event generation and merging

The reason the pipeline renders at high frame rate is the event side. v2e converts
a frame sequence into events; applied to standard-rate RGB it must interpolate
between frames, which fabricates or destroys the sub-frame flake motion.

quote:
By generating intermediate frames with a slow-motion
factor of 8,
resolution is substantially
enhanced, thereby enabling more precise event generation.

claim_text: Reducing the framerate of the source video by a factor of 80 drastically reduces the downstream test score, as artifacts caused by the low frame rate become apparent.
quote:
